summary: Fowler describes an enterprise SOA challenge where consumer services need functionality that supplier services haven't yet exposed. The traditional approach creates bottlenecks as supplier teams have competing priorities. He presents a solution observed at a client: treating internal services as open source projects with designated custodians who review patches from consumer developers. This model reduces friction by letting consumer teams write their own changes while custodians maintain quality control. The approach mirrors open source governance, where reviewing patches requires less effort than implementing changes, and trusted contributors can eventually become committers.
key_points: In SOA environments, supplier service teams often can't prioritize features needed by consumer teams, creating organizational friction
The internal open source model lets consumer developers write patches for supplier services, with custodians reviewing and committing changes
Reviewing a patch is much less effort than making a change yourself, significantly reducing bottlenecks
Consumer developers can graduate to committer status once custodians trust their work, further reducing friction
Fancy service registry products can be replaced with simpler humane registries
money_quotes: They took a leaf out of the open source play-book and made all their services into internal open source systems.
Each service has a couple of custodians - people whose responsibility it is to keep the service in a healthy state.
Just like an open-source maintainer, the custodian receives the patch and reviews it to see if it's good enough to commit.
Reviewing a patch is much less effort than making a change yourself.
A consumer developer can be made a committer once the custodians are comfortable.
key_insight: Applying open source governance patterns internally—with service custodians reviewing patches from consumer teams—reduces organizational bottlenecks in service-oriented architectures far more effectively than traditional ownership models.
